name: It is written: the temptation's scriptures
notes: When the tempter assails the Son of God in the wilderness, the contest is fought with the written Word. To every assault the Lord answers "It is written," and each answer is drawn from Deuteronomy: that man lives by every word of God (Deut 8:3), that the LORD is not to be tempted (Deut 6:16), and that Him only shall man worship and serve (Deut 6:13). The tempter, in turn, also quotes Scripture, urging the Lord to cast Himself down by citing the angels' charge of Psalm 91:11-12 -- yet he leaves off "to keep thee in all thy ways," wresting the promise to a presumption it never bore. This weave links each Old Testament source to the Gospel verses that quote it, both the Lord's three rebuttals and the adversary's single citation. Matthew and Luke order the second and third temptations differently and Luke shortens the bread answer, vantages left as a matter for further study; both bear the same victory of the Word over the wilderness.
